quiz.title: Unit 9 Quiz 3, Grade 11 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Genetics: Mendel's Experiments and Laws of Inheritance
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Pea Plant Experiments, P, F1, F2 Generations, True-Breeding, Monohybrid Cross, Law of Segregation, Law of Independent Assortment, Law of Dominance

questions.question: Gregor Mendel used which organism for his genetic experiments?
questions.explanation: Mendel used pea plants (Pisum sativum) for his genetic experiments because they had distinct traits, were easy to grow, and could be controlled for cross-pollination. Fruit flies were used by Morgan, mice by other researchers, and bacteria for molecular genetics.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Mendel's experiments focused on traits that were:
questions.explanation: Mendel focused on discrete traits (clear-cut alternatives like tall/short, yellow/green) that were heritable. He avoided continuous traits (like height in humans) and polygenic traits, and controlled for environmental effects.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which of the following traits did Mendel study?
questions.explanation: Mendel studied seven traits in pea plants: plant height (tall/short), seed shape (round/wrinkled), seed color (yellow/green), flower color (purple/white), pod shape (inflated/constricted), pod color (green/yellow), and flower position (axial/terminal). He did not study leaf arrangement, root length, or stem thickness.

questions.question: The parent plants in Mendel's experiments are called:
questions.explanation: P generation (parental generation) are the original parent plants in Mendel's crosses. F1 (first filial) are their offspring, and F2 (second filial) are produced by crossing F1 plants.

questions.question: The first generation offspring of Mendel's cross are called:
questions.explanation: F1 generation (first filial) are the offspring of the P generation cross. They are hybrids showing only dominant traits in Mendel's experiments.

questions.question: Mendel allowed the F1 plants to self-pollinate to produce:
questions.explanation: Self-pollination of F1 plants produced the F2 generation, where recessive traits reappeared in a 3:1 ratio (dominant:recessive).

questions.question: A test cross involves crossing an individual with:
questions.explanation: A test cross involves crossing an individual with an unknown genotype (but dominant phenotype) with a homozygous recessive individual. The offspring ratios reveal whether the unknown parent is homozygous dominant or heterozygous. If any offspring show recessive traits, the unknown parent must be heterozygous.

questions.question: Mendel's experiments supported which view of inheritance?
questions.explanation: Mendel's experiments supported the particulate theory of inheritance, which states that traits are inherited as discrete units (genes) that remain unchanged from generation to generation. This contradicted the blending theory (traits blend like paint), Lamarckism (acquired characteristics inherited), and pangenesis (body particles influence gametes).

questions.question: In Mendel's experiments, a trait that disappeared in the F1 generation but reappeared in the F2 generation is called:
questions.explanation: Recessive traits disappeared in F1 (masked by dominant) but reappeared in F2 when plants were homozygous recessive. Dominant traits appeared in both generations.

questions.question: Which of these is a monohybrid cross?
options: Crossing two plants differing in one trait
Crossing two plants differing in two traits
Crossing plants with more than three traits
questions.explanation: A monohybrid cross involves parents differing in one trait (e.g., tall × short). Dihybrid crosses involve two traits (e.g., tall round × short wrinkled).

questions.question: Which description accurately defines the biological nature of the lineage Mendel referred to as true-breeding?
options: Vegetation that consistently generates a diverse range of hybrid characteristics.
Organisms that produce offspring possessing traits identical to their own kind.
Living things that maintain a mixed set of different alleles for a single feature.
Plants that undergo the process of self-pollination in a completely erratic fashion.
questions.explanation: True-breeding organisms are homozygous for traits and produce offspring identical to themselves when self-pollinated. They do not produce hybrids or mixed alleles.

questions.question: According to the Law of Segregation, each gamete receives:
questions.explanation: Law of Segregation states that allele pairs separate during gamete formation, so each gamete carries only one allele for each gene. Fertilization restores the pair.

questions.question: Which statement accurately describes the biological principle known as the Law of Independent Assortment?
options: The alleles for different traits segregate independently during gamete formation.
The dominant alleles consistently undergo the process of segregation before others.
The recessive alleles remain concealed behind the presence of a dominant variant.
The inherited traits combine together to create a blended physical appearance.
questions.explanation: Independent assortment means alleles for different genes segregate independently during gamete formation, producing various combinations. This applies to genes on different chromosomes.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a law proposed by Mendel?
questions.explanation: Law of Crossing Over was discovered later (by Morgan) and describes recombination during meiosis. Mendel's three laws are Segregation, Independent Assortment, and Dominance.

questions.question: How many fundamental laws of inheritance did Gregor Mendel postulate?
questions.explanation: Mendel proposed three fundamental laws: Law of Segregation, Law of Independent Assortment, and Law of Dominance. These form the basis of classical genetics.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered Mendel's experiments and laws! 🌱
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the generations (P, F1, F2) and their relationships.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on true-breeding and monohybrid crosses.
scoreMessages.0: Please review Mendel's pea plant traits, generations, and three laws of inheritance.
